config.title: Robotics: From Foundations to Advanced Kinematics
config.description: A deep dive into robotic subsystems, spatial representation, forward and inverse kinematics, and differential motion control.

questions.question: An industrial robot is primarily distinguished from fixed automation by its ability to be reprogrammed via software.
questions.explanation: Industrial robots are defined by their flexibility; software changes allow them to perform different tasks without hardware rebuilding.
questions.hint: Think about whether a machine needs a physical overhaul to change its job.

questions.question: The 'Manipulator' subsystem of a robot acts as the muscles in the professional athlete analogy.
questions.explanation: In the athlete analogy, the Manipulator is the skeleton (structure), while the Actuators are the muscles.
questions.hint: Which part provides the physical structure vs. the power?

questions.question: Proprioceptive sensors are used to monitor the robot's own internal state, such as joint angles.
questions.explanation: Proprioceptive sensors like encoders measure internal variables like position and velocity of the robot's own joints.
questions.hint: Does 'proprio-' refer to internal or external sensing?

questions.question: A SCARA robot typically has three revolute joints and no prismatic joints.
questions.explanation: SCARA robots generally have two parallel revolute joints for horizontal motion and one prismatic joint for vertical motion.
questions.hint: Recall the joint configuration used for fast vertical assembly.

questions.question: A position vector in 3D space is standardly represented as a 1x3 row matrix in robotic calculations.
questions.explanation: Position vectors are standardly represented as 3x1 column vectors containing X, Y, and Z coordinates.
questions.hint: Is it a row or a column for standard matrix multiplication?

questions.question: Matrix multiplication for rotations is commutative, meaning the order of rotations does not matter.
questions.explanation: Rotations are non-commutative; changing the order of multiplication results in a different final orientation.
questions.hint: Try to imagine rotating a book 90 degrees around X then Y, vs Y then X.

questions.question: Forward Kinematics involves finding joint angles when the end-effector's position is known.
questions.explanation: That is Inverse Kinematics. Forward Kinematics calculates the pose based on known joint variables.
questions.hint: Which one goes from 'angles' to 'position'?

questions.question: In Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters, 'theta' is the joint variable for a prismatic joint.
questions.explanation: Theta is the variable for revolute joints; 'd' (offset) is the variable for prismatic joints.
questions.hint: Does theta represent an angle or a distance?

questions.question: Analytical (closed-form) solutions for Inverse Kinematics are generally slower than numerical iterative methods.
questions.explanation: Analytical solutions provide direct equations and are much faster and more precise than iterative numerical methods.
questions.hint: Think about whether solving a direct formula is faster than guessing and checking.

questions.question: A 2-link robot can have at most one solution for reaching a specific point within its workspace.
questions.explanation: A 2-link robot often has two solutions: elbow-up and elbow-down configurations.
questions.hint: Can you reach the same point on a table with your elbow high or low?

questions.question: The Jacobian matrix relates joint velocities to end-effector Cartesian velocities.
questions.explanation: The Jacobian provides the linear mapping that converts joint space speeds into task space speeds.
questions.hint: This matrix is the 'velocity map' of the robot.

questions.question: At a singularity, a robot gains an extra degree of freedom to move in any direction.
questions.explanation: At a singularity, a robot loses one or more degrees of freedom and cannot move in certain directions.
questions.hint: Is a singularity a position of power or a position of limitation?

questions.question: If the determinant of the Jacobian matrix is zero, the robot is in a singular configuration.
questions.explanation: Mathematically, a singularity occurs when the Jacobian matrix loses full rank, which is indicated by a zero determinant.
questions.hint: Recall the mathematical condition for a matrix to be non-invertible.

questions.question: Kinematic Decoupling is primarily used to separate the base control from the sensor control.
questions.explanation: Kinematic Decoupling (Pieper's Method) separates the position problem (first 3 joints) from the orientation problem (wrist).
questions.hint: Does decoupling refer to hardware components or mathematical problems?

questions.question: The 'Mapping' role of a transformation matrix involves physically moving a point to a new location in the same frame.
questions.explanation: Mapping converts coordinates from one reference frame to another without moving the physical point.
questions.hint: Is it a translation of the point or a translation of the description?

questions.question: How many Degrees of Freedom (DoF) are generally required to define both position and orientation in 3D space?
questions.explanation: Six DoF are required: 3 for spatial location (X, Y, Z) and 3 for orientation (rotation axes).
questions.hint: Count the axes of movement and the axes of rotation needed for full freedom.

questions.question: Which subsystem is described as the 'brain' of the robot?
questions.explanation: The controller processes data and issues commands, effectively acting as the robot's brain.
questions.hint: Which part handles the 'thinking' and 'deciding'?

questions.question: What does 'TCP' stand for in robotics?
questions.explanation: TCP stands for Tool Center Point, the specific point on the end-effector that follows the path.
questions.hint: It's the very tip of the robot's tool.

questions.question: Which joint type provides linear or sliding movement?
questions.explanation: Prismatic joints allow translational (sliding) movement along a single axis.
questions.hint: Think of opening a drawer.

questions.question: Why did industrial robots see a rise in adoption since the 1960s?
questions.explanation: Robotics grew due to the need for cost reduction (economics) and removing humans from dangerous environments (safety).
questions.hint: Consider the benefits of replacing human labor in hazards.

questions.question: What is the standard size of a Homogeneous Transformation Matrix?
questions.explanation: A homogeneous matrix is 4x4, combining a 3x3 rotation matrix and a 3x1 translation vector.
questions.hint: It needs extra room for the translation components.

questions.question: In the athlete analogy, what do the sensors represent?
questions.explanation: Sensors provide feedback like biological nerves and eyes, allowing the robot to 'feel' or 'see' its state.
questions.hint: What parts of your body give you feedback about your environment?

questions.question: Which of these is a revolute joint?
questions.explanation: Revolute joints provide rotational motion, like a hinge or a human elbow.
questions.hint: Look for the option that involves turning or spinning.

questions.question: Which coordinate frame is fixed and serves as the reference for all other frames?
questions.explanation: The Base Frame (Frame 0) is attached to the non-moving part of the robot and is the root of the kinematic chain.
questions.hint: It's the frame at the bottom of the robot.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of kinematic modeling?

options.label: B
options.text: To describe motion without regarding forces

options.label: C
options.text: To determine the weight of the robot

questions.explanation: Kinematics is the study of motion (position, velocity, acceleration) without considering the forces that cause it.
questions.hint: Does it care about 'how' it moves or the 'forces' moving it?

questions.question: What is the 'DNA of the robot' in modeling?
questions.explanation: The DH table contains all the geometric constants and joint variables needed to describe the robot's structure.
questions.hint: It's the standard table used in forward kinematics.

questions.question: Which tool is typically recommended for 6-DoF matrix calculations to avoid manual errors?
questions.explanation: Computational software like MATLAB is essential for handling the heavy 4x4 matrix math of high-DoF robots.
questions.hint: Think of advanced engineering software.

questions.question: What happens if a robot link is not treated as a rigid body?
questions.explanation: Standard kinematics assumes rigid bodies (no bending) to simplify geometry; otherwise, deformation math is required.
questions.hint: Does bending make math easier or harder?

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a main component of a robot manipulator?
questions.explanation: While specialized, an atmospheric sensor is not a core functional component like actuators or controllers.
questions.hint: Which one isn't strictly necessary for the robot to move and function?

questions.question: How many joints are typically required for an industrial manipulator to have 6 DoF?
questions.explanation: In open kinematic chains, each joint usually provides one degree of freedom; thus 6 joints provide 6 DoF.
questions.hint: One joint per degree of freedom is the standard rule.

questions.question: What is a key property of an orthogonal rotation matrix?
questions.explanation: For rotation matrices, R^-1 = R^T, which makes calculating the inverse extremely efficient.
questions.hint: Swapping rows and columns gives you the inverse.

questions.question: Which representation uses only 3 parameters to specify orientation for better human visualization?
questions.explanation: Euler angles (Roll, Pitch, Yaw) use three angles to represent orientation instead of a complex 3x3 matrix.
questions.hint: It's often called Roll, Pitch, and Yaw.

questions.question: In a Homogeneous Transformation Matrix, which part represents the 3D position?
questions.explanation: The top-right 3x1 column of a 4x4 homogeneous matrix contains the translation (X, Y, Z) coordinates.
questions.hint: Orientation is in the 3x3, but position is in the side column.

questions.question: If you rotate about the X-axis by 90 degrees and then the Y-axis by 90 degrees, is the result same as Y then X?

options.label: B
options.text: No, because rotations do not commute

options.label: D
options.text: Only if the robot is SCARA

questions.explanation: Rotations are non-commutative; the order of matrix multiplication changes the final orientation.
questions.hint: Recall the matrix property AB != BA.

questions.question: What does a rotation matrix determinant of +1 indicate?

options.label: C
options.text: The robot is at its max reach

questions.explanation: A determinant of +1 is a requirement for a proper rotation matrix in a right-handed coordinate system.
questions.hint: This is a fundamental property of orthonormal matrices.

questions.question: Which DH parameter represents the distance between joint axes measured along the common normal?
questions.explanation: 'a' represents the link length, the physical distance between two adjacent joint axes.
questions.hint: It's the constant length of the metal link itself.

questions.question: Which DH parameter represents the 'twist' between two Z-axes?
questions.explanation: Alpha (α) is the link twist, defining the angle between the Z-axes of two consecutive joints.
questions.hint: It's the angular version of link length.

questions.question: In a serial robot, how do you find the total transformation from base to end-effector?

options.label: B
options.text: Multiply all individual A-matrices in order

options.label: C
options.text: Calculate the determinant of the base

options.label: D
options.text: Subtract the tool frame from the base

questions.explanation: The total transformation is the product of the individual transformation matrices: T = A1 * A2 * ... * An.
questions.hint: It's a chain of multiplications.

questions.question: What is the result of a Forward Kinematics calculation?
questions.explanation: Forward kinematics results in the end-effector pose (position and orientation) relative to the base.
questions.hint: Where is the hand located?

questions.question: Why is Inverse Kinematics (IK) considered more difficult than Forward Kinematics?

options.label: B
options.text: It involves non-linear equations and multiple solutions

options.label: D
options.text: It is not used in industry

questions.explanation: IK is hard because it requires solving non-linear trigonometric equations which can result in multiple valid configurations.
questions.hint: Think about whether there's more than one way to reach a point.

questions.question: For a two-link robot with lengths L1 and L2, what is the maximum reach?
questions.explanation: The maximum outer boundary is the sum of the two link lengths when the arm is fully stretched.
questions.hint: The arm is fully extended in a straight line.

questions.question: What is 'Elbow-Up' and 'Elbow-Down' an example of?
questions.explanation: These are the two common configurations (solutions) for a single target point in simple arm kinematics.
questions.hint: Two ways to reach the same goal.

questions.question: What happens if you try to solve IK for a point outside the workspace?
questions.explanation: Targets outside the workspace are physically unreachable, causing mathematical equations to fail (e.g., cos(theta) > 1).
questions.hint: Can you calculate the angle to a point you can't touch?

questions.question: Which matrix is used to identify if a robot is at a singularity?
questions.explanation: The Jacobian matrix is analyzed; if its determinant is zero, the robot is in a singularity.
questions.hint: This matrix maps velocities.

questions.question: What is a physical symptom of a robot approaching a singularity?

options.label: B
options.text: Joint velocities spike to near-infinite levels

questions.explanation: As the robot loses mobility in a direction, the math requires infinite joint speed to move even slightly in that direction.
questions.hint: The motors will suddenly spin extremely fast.

questions.question: A SCARA robot is specifically optimized for what kind of tasks?
questions.explanation: SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) is built for fast horizontal moves and vertical insertion/assembly.
questions.hint: Think of picking and placing parts on a conveyor belt.

questions.question: What distinguishes an 'open-loop' kinematic chain (like Niryo Ned 2)?

options.label: B
options.text: It has no returning loops or parallel links

questions.explanation: Open-loop (serial) chains move in one direction from base to tip with no physical cycles or loops.
questions.hint: It's like a single line of links from the ground to the tool.

questions.question: How many DH parameters are defined per joint?
questions.explanation: The DH convention reduces robot geometry to four parameters per link/joint pair (theta, d, a, alpha).
questions.hint: Count θ, d, a, α.

questions.question: In DH parameters, 'd' represents what?
questions.explanation: 'd' is the joint offset, measured as the distance between X-axes along the Z-axis.
questions.hint: It's the distance along the joint's axis of motion.

questions.question: What kind of singularity occurs when the robot is at its minimum inner reach limit?
questions.explanation: A fully folded singularity occurs when the arm moves as close to its base as possible, losing motion capability.
questions.hint: Think of the robot arm collapsing in on itself.

questions.question: What is the result of multiplying the Jacobian transpose by a force vector at the end-effector?
questions.explanation: The Jacobian transpose relates Cartesian forces at the tip to the torques required at the joints (τ = J^T * F).
questions.hint: It's the 'Static' version of the velocity mapping.

questions.question: Which singularity type is commonly found in 6-DoF manipulators with three intersecting wrist axes?
questions.explanation: A wrist singularity occurs when two of the wrist axes align, causing the robot to lose a degree of freedom in orientation.
questions.hint: It happens specifically in the 'hand' area of the robot.

questions.question: Which Euler angle convention involves rotations about moving axes rather than fixed ones?
questions.explanation: Intrinsic Euler angles rotate about the current (moving) coordinate system axes, not the original fixed frame.
questions.hint: The axes move along with the object as it rotates.

questions.question: In the DH convention, the Z-axis for frame {i} is always aligned with what?

options.label: B
options.text: The axis of motion for joint {i+1}

questions.explanation: The standard DH convention places the Z-axis of a frame along the axis of motion (rotation or translation) of the next joint.
questions.hint: It's the line the motor spins around.

questions.question: How does Kinematic Decoupling simplify a 6-DoF robot's IK?

options.label: B
options.text: By solving a 3-DoF position problem and a 3-DoF orientation problem separately

options.label: D
options.text: By assuming all links are the same length

questions.explanation: It splits the 6-DoF problem into two smaller 3-DoF problems: the 'arm' for position and the 'wrist' for orientation.
questions.hint: Divide and conquer the position and orientation.

questions.question: What is the mathematical definition of a singularity in terms of matrix rank?
questions.explanation: A singularity is a point where the Jacobian matrix loses rank (rank deficiency), meaning some directions of motion are no longer possible.
questions.hint: Think about when a matrix doesn't have enough 'info' to be inverted.

questions.question: When building a DH table, what is the role of the 'common normal'?

options.label: B
options.text: It is the shortest distance between two skew lines (Z-axes)

options.label: D
options.text: It is used for gravity compensation

questions.explanation: The common normal is the line perpendicular to two consecutive Z-axes; its length is the DH parameter 'a'.
questions.hint: The shortest path between two non-intersecting lines.

questions.question: What is the inverse of a 4x4 Homogeneous Transformation Matrix T?

options.label: B
options.text: A special structure using R^T and -R^T*P

options.label: D
options.text: A matrix of zeros and ones

questions.explanation: Because it's a block matrix, the inverse requires transposing the rotation part and calculating a new translation vector based on that transpose.
questions.hint: You can't just transpose the whole 4x4; you transpose only the 3x3 part.

questions.question: If a robot skeleton (manipulator) is too heavy for its muscles (actuators), what is the physical result?

options.label: B
options.text: The robot will be slow and sluggish

questions.explanation: According to the athlete analogy, a heavy skeleton paired with weak muscles leads to a slow, underpowered system.
questions.hint: Think of a heavy person with small muscles trying to run.

questions.question: What happens to the manipulability measure (Yoshikawa) at a singularity?
questions.explanation: Manipulability measures how easily the robot can move in all directions; it drops to zero at a singularity where one or more directions are lost.
questions.hint: Think about 'ability' going away completely.

questions.question: Which method is used to help a robot 'choose' between multiple valid IK solutions?

options.label: D
options.text: Shutting down if more than one exists

questions.explanation: Engineers program logic to select the best solution based on which is closer to the current pose or which avoids hitting obstacles.
questions.hint: It needs a rule to decide which path is safer or shorter.

questions.question: Industrial robots adoption is estimated to save employers what percentage of labor costs by 2025?
questions.explanation: Estimates suggest a 16% saving on labor costs through robotic automation by 2025.
questions.hint: It's a significant double-digit number under 20.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'bottom row [0 0 0 1]' in a Homogeneous Matrix?

options.label: B
options.text: To enable matrix multiplication of 4x4 with 4x1 vectors

questions.explanation: The extra row makes the matrix square, allowing for the compounding of rotation and translation through standard multiplication.
questions.hint: It turns the 3D math into a 4D linear operation for convenience.

questions.question: In the Niryo Ned 2, how many rotating joints are there?
questions.explanation: The Niryo Ned 2 is a 6-axis (6-DoF) collaborative robot arm.
questions.hint: It's a standard educational 6-axis arm.

questions.question: What happens if you multiply A1 * A2 in a different order (A2 * A1)?
questions.explanation: Since matrix multiplication is non-commutative, the chain of frames would be built differently, resulting in a different pose.
questions.hint: Order matters in transformation chains.

questions.question: Which IK solution type is found by guessing and iterating toward an answer?
questions.explanation: Numerical methods use iterative algorithms (like Newton-Raphson) to find an approximate solution when a direct equation isn't possible.
questions.hint: It repeats the calculation over and over.

questions.question: What is the Jacobian for a 1-DoF system with length L and angle theta moving in 2D?
questions.explanation: By differentiating x = L cosθ and y = L sinθ with respect to θ, you get dx = -L sinθ dθ and dy = L cosθ dθ.
questions.hint: Use calculus to differentiate the position coordinates.

questions.question: A 'fixed automation' system differs from a robot because it is:
questions.explanation: Fixed automation is designed for one task; changing that task requires physical rebuilding, unlike a programmable robot.
questions.hint: Think of a machine that only does one thing forever.

questions.question: What type of actuators are most popular in industrial robotics?
questions.explanation: Electric motors are the standard due to ease of control, cleanliness, and precise feedback.
questions.hint: It's the most common clean energy source for small motion.

questions.question: How is the 'orientation' of frame B relative to A represented in a rotation matrix?

options.label: B
options.text: By unit vectors of B's axes expressed in A's coordinates as columns

options.label: C
options.text: By the length of the links

questions.explanation: The columns of the 3x3 rotation matrix are the unit vectors X, Y, and Z of the target frame expressed in the reference frame.
questions.hint: Stack the directions of the axes.

questions.question: In the formula A_i = Rot_z(θ) * Trans_z(d) * Trans_x(a) * Rot_x(α), which part represents the rotation between Z-axes?
questions.explanation: Rot_x(α) represents the link twist, which is the angle between the Z-axes measured about the X-axis.
questions.hint: Alpha is the twist angle.

questions.question: What algebraic error signifies you have asked a robot to reach beyond its max reach?

options.label: B
options.text: Square root of a negative number or cos(θ) > 1

questions.explanation: Mathematically, the law of cosines will result in a value outside the [-1, 1] range for cos(θ), making the angle calculation impossible.
questions.hint: Trigonometry only works within certain bounds.

questions.question: How do you handle a singularity in robot control to avoid infinite velocity commands?

options.label: B
options.text: Damped Least Squares (DLS) or virtual boundaries

questions.explanation: Damped Least Squares limits the joint speeds near singularities at the cost of some tracking accuracy, while virtual boundaries prevent entry.
questions.hint: You need a mathematical 'brake' or a physical 'fence'.

questions.question: According to the sources, why would a robot shake?

options.label: B
options.text: If the muscles are too strong for the skeleton

options.label: D
options.text: If it has too many joints

questions.explanation: In the athlete analogy, if actuators are too powerful for the rigidity of the manipulator, it leads to vibration and shaking.
questions.hint: Think of a super-powered motor on a thin, flimsy stick.

questions.question: Which coordinate frame in the Niryo-NED2 represents the actual task point for pick-and-place?
questions.explanation: The End-Effector Frame {EE} is attached to the gripper and is the frame actually used to interact with objects.
questions.hint: It's the 'hand' frame.

questions.question: What is the primary function of an actuator?
questions.explanation: Actuators (like motors) take electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy and produce torque or force.
questions.hint: It's the part that actually 'acts' or moves.

questions.question: A position sensor like an optical encoder is typically found where?
questions.explanation: Encoders are usually attached to the motor shafts to measure the exact rotation angle of each joint.
questions.hint: It needs to measure the motor's spin.

questions.question: Which robot type is based on three linear (prismatic) axes?
questions.explanation: Cartesian robots move in X, Y, and Z directions using linear joints, creating a rectangular workspace.
questions.hint: It uses the Cartesian coordinate system directly.

questions.question: How many vectors locate the origin and describe orientation in a robotic 'Frame'?
questions.explanation: A frame is a set of four vectors: one position vector for the origin and three unit vectors for orientation.
questions.hint: 1 for where it is + 3 for where it's pointing.

questions.question: Which field studies motion without regard to the forces that cause it?
questions.explanation: Kinematics is purely the geometric study of motion over time.
questions.hint: It comes from the Greek word for 'motion'.

questions.question: What is the typical strategy for solving the IK of a 6-DoF arm with a spherical wrist?

options.label: B
options.text: Solve position of the wrist center first, then solve orientation

options.label: C
options.text: Guess the first 3 and calculate the rest

questions.explanation: By solving for the wrist center (first 3 joints), you simplify the problem to an orientation problem (last 3 joints).
questions.hint: Find the center of the hand before deciding how to turn it.

questions.question: What does a zero determinant in a 3x3 Jacobian for position imply?

options.label: A
options.text: The robot is at the base

options.label: B
options.text: The robot cannot move in at least one Cartesian direction

options.label: C
options.text: The robot has reached its maximum speed

questions.explanation: A zero determinant means the Jacobian is non-invertible, signifying a loss of mobility in certain directions.
questions.hint: It's a mathematical wall.

questions.question: Why is the Z-Y-X Euler angle representation called 'intrinsic'?

options.label: B
options.text: Because rotations occur about axes of the moving system

options.label: C
options.text: Because it uses only integer values

options.label: D
options.text: Because it's the only way to represent 3D

questions.explanation: Intrinsic rotations are defined relative to the current, moving frame which changes with each rotation.
questions.hint: The coordinate system 'follows' the object's movement.

questions.question: What is a major limitation of using a DH table for modeling?

options.label: B
options.text: It cannot handle parallel robots or closed loops easily

options.label: D
options.text: It is not used by engineers

questions.explanation: Standard DH parameters are designed for serial 'open' chains and become very complex for 'closed' parallel chains.
questions.hint: Serial vs Parallel.

questions.question: How does the Analytical Jacobian differ from the Geometric Jacobian?

options.label: A
options.text: One uses degrees and the other uses radians

options.label: B
options.text: Analytical depends on the orientation parameterization used (e.g. Euler angles)

questions.explanation: The Geometric Jacobian is based on twist/cross products, while the Analytical Jacobian is the partial derivative of the pose with respect to joints.
questions.hint: One is derived from physics, the other from calculus of angles.

questions.question: In the 2R Planar Robot example, what represents the orientation of the second link?
questions.explanation: The orientation of the second link is the cumulative sum of all joint angles leading up to it.
questions.hint: The angles add up along the arm.

questions.question: Why do we use frame assignments in Niryo-NED2 modeling?

options.label: B
options.text: To provide a common language between hardware and math

options.label: C
options.text: To reduce the weight of the robot

questions.explanation: Frames allow us to map physical joint rotations into mathematical transformation matrices consistently.
questions.hint: It's the bridge between the physical and digital world.

questions.question: What is the 'inner circle' limit for a 2-link robot?
questions.explanation: The inner limit of the workspace is the absolute difference between the link lengths where the arm is fully folded.
questions.hint: Think of the closest point the robot can reach to its own base.

questions.question: If you have 6 joints and 6 Cartesian variables, the Jacobian is a:
questions.explanation: A 6-DoF robot moving in 6D task space has a 6x6 Jacobian matrix mapping joint speeds to spatial velocities.
questions.hint: It relates 6 joint speeds to 6 task speeds.

questions.question: The Niryo NED 2 is an example of what kind of robot?
questions.explanation: It has 6 revolute joints resembling a human arm, making it an articulated manipulator.
questions.hint: It looks and moves like a human arm.

questions.question: In the DH transformation matrix, which column represents the Z-axis of the current frame relative to the previous?
questions.explanation: The first three columns of a rotation matrix represent the X, Y, and Z unit vectors respectively.
questions.hint: X is 1, Y is 2, Z is 3.

questions.question: Why are 'elbow-up' and 'elbow-down' configurations a problem for automated path planning?

options.label: B
options.text: Sudden flips between configurations can cause damage

options.label: C
options.text: They use different amounts of electricity

questions.explanation: Switching from up to down requires the arm to pass through a singularity or move significantly, which can be dangerous.
questions.hint: Sudden big movements are bad for high-speed industrial robots.

questions.question: What is the relationship between joint torques (τ) and end-effector force (F) using the Jacobian (J)?
questions.explanation: Based on the principle of virtual work, joint torques are equal to the Jacobian transpose multiplied by the force vector.
questions.hint: It involves the transpose of the velocity map.

questions.question: A transformation matrix $^A_BT$ converts coordinates from frame B to A. What is $^B_AT$?
questions.explanation: To reverse the coordinate conversion, you must use the inverse transformation matrix.
questions.hint: Reverse mapping requires inverting the math.

questions.question: Which field focuses on the forces and torques required to cause motion?
questions.explanation: Dynamics involves the study of the physical forces and torques that result in the robot's motion.
questions.hint: It's the 'force' side of the physics of motion.

questions.question: In the standard DH formula, which rotation happens LAST?
questions.explanation: The standard sequence is: rotate θ about Z, translate d along Z, translate a along X, and finally rotate α about X.
questions.hint: Alpha is the final 'twist' parameter.

questions.question: What is the primary role of a robot's 'End-Effector'?
questions.explanation: The end-effector is the 'hand' of the robot (gripper, welder, tool) that actually performs the work.
questions.hint: It's the very end of the kinematic chain.

questions.question: Euler angles are used to represent orientation with 3 parameters. What is a common disadvantage?
questions.explanation: Euler angles suffer from gimbal lock, where two of the three rotation axes align and a degree of freedom is lost.
questions.hint: Think of a physical set of gimbals getting stuck.

questions.question: Which joint configuration is used to reach into confined spaces?
questions.explanation: Articulated robots have human-like flexibility, allowing them to bend and reach into tight areas where bulky gantries cannot go.
questions.hint: Think of how your own arm can reach behind things.

questions.question: A Jacobian matrix that is not square (e.g., 6x3) implies the robot is:
questions.explanation: If the number of joints does not match the number of task variables, the Jacobian is non-square.
questions.hint: It means the robot doesn't have 6 joints for 6 tasks.

flashcards.definition: The number of independent variables required to specify the position and orientation of all parts of a mechanism.
A reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material or tools through variable programmed motions.
The 'muscles' of the robot that convert electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into physical motion.
The 'brain' of the robotic system that processes sensor data and executes control algorithms to command actuators.
The device at the end of a robotic arm (like a gripper or welding torch) designed to interact with the environment.
A joint that allows rotational motion between two links around a single fixed axis.
A joint that allows linear or translational sliding motion between two links.
An idealization of a solid body in which deformation is neglected, keeping link lengths constant during motion.
A 3x3 orthonormal matrix used to represent the orientation of one coordinate frame relative to another.
A 4x4 matrix combining a 3x3 rotation matrix and a 3x1 translation vector for complete spatial description.
A matrix where columns are unit vectors perpendicular to each other; its inverse is equal to its transpose.
A set of three angles (e.g., Roll, Pitch, Yaw) used to describe orientation in 3D space.
The mathematical property where the order of operations matters (e.g., A*B != B*A), critical in sequential rotations.
The fixed coordinate system at the root of the robot, usually denoted as Frame {0}.
The specific point on the end-effector used as the reference for trajectory and positioning tasks.
The use of a transformation matrix to express the coordinates of a point in a different reference frame.
The process of calculating the position and orientation of the end-effector from known joint variables.
A standardized method for attaching coordinate frames to links and modeling robot kinematics using four parameters.
The DH parameter representing the distance between joint axes measured along the common normal.
The DH parameter representing the angle between the Z-axes of two consecutive joints.
The DH parameter representing the rotation of a revolute joint; it is the variable in revolute kinematics.
The DH parameter representing the distance between X-axes along the joint Z-axis; the variable for prismatic joints.
A series of links connected by joints; can be open (serial) or closed (parallel).
The individual 4x4 transformation matrix derived from DH parameters for a single link.
The process of calculating the joint variables required to reach a specific desired end-effector pose.
An analytical mathematical equation that provides exact results directly without iteration.
The occurrence of multiple valid joint configurations (e.g., elbow-up/down) that reach the same target pose.
The total volume of space a robot can physically reach with its end-effector.
A target pose outside the robot's workspace, resulting in mathematical errors in IK solvers.
A technique to simplify 6-DoF IK by separating the position problem from the orientation problem.
The two common geometric configurations for a 2-link manipulator reaching a specific point.
A wrist design where all three axes intersect at a single point, greatly simplifying IK calculations.
A matrix mapping joint velocities to end-effector Cartesian velocities.
A configuration where the robot loses a degree of freedom and the Jacobian becomes non-invertible.
The near-infinite joint velocity required to move the end-effector near a singular point.
A singularity occurring when the robot is at its maximum outer reach (fully stretched).
A singularity occurring when two or more joint axes align within the workspace.
The relationship between joint torques and end-effector forces using the Jacobian transpose.
A mathematical indicator used to detect singularities; when it equals zero, the robot is singular.
A 6x1 vector representing the combined linear and angular velocity of a rigid body.

kinematics-inverse.definition: Separating pose into position and orientation

achievements.title: Rookie Mechanic
achievements.description: Complete your first revision session in practice mode.

achievements.title: Spatial Wizard
achievements.description: Achieve 100% accuracy in the spatial representation category.

achievements.title: DH Specialist
achievements.description: Answer 20 Forward Kinematics questions correctly in a row.

achievements.title: Speed Demon
achievements.description: Complete a speedrun session in under 120 seconds.

achievements.title: Jacobian Master
achievements.description: Correctly identify all singularity types without using hints.

achievements.title: Hardcore Survivalist
achievements.description: Complete the Survival mode with at least 50 correct answers.

achievements.title: Full Stack Engineer
achievements.description: Correctly answer at least one question from every category.

achievements.title: Precision Expert
achievements.description: Finish Blitz mode with an accuracy over 90%.

achievements.title: Flashcard Guru
achievements.description: Review all flashcards in the deck.

achievements.title: Robot Overlord
achievements.description: Unlock all other achievements to master the field of robotics.
